# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for emotionally gentle movies with beautiful visuals—no stress, no violence

**Score:** 44
**Body:** i’m in the mood for films that feel like warm hugs calm pacing, thoughtful dialogue, maybe poetic or artistic but without intense tragedy or plot twists.
think: The Lunchbox, Patterson, Before Sunrise.
more like these?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Perfect Days (2023)
2. Moonrise Kingdom, Amelie
3. Under the Tuscan Sun
  - Waking Ned Divine. I got this recommendation recently and after watching it - loved it :)
4. Local Hero.
5. Columbus (2017) or After Yang (2021) both by Kogonada, both calm and beautiful.
6. Paddington
7. Pride and Prejudice, A Good Year, anything by Nancy Meyers.
8. The Ballad of Wallis Island (2025)
9. Cinema Paradiso

## 2. Looking for a Good Michael Shannon Movie

**Score:** 49
**Body:** I’m watching Boardwalk Empire right now and realizing how much I like Michael Shannon as an actor. Only other thing I’ve really seen him in is The Shape of Water and thought he was excellent in that too. What are some other intense characters he has played in film?

EDIT: Thank you for all the recs! Lots of movies I haven’t heard of and excited to check out. I’m going to start off with Take Shelter tonight :)

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I LOVED him in Bug (2006). Would absolutely recommend that one if you want intense characters lol
2. Take Shelter. It’s a GREAT flick.
  - This one sticks with me the most.
  - second this
3. Knives Out.  He's great in that movie.
4. [The Iceman](https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/the_iceman_2013) (2012)
5. Michael Shannon reads the Insane Delta Gamma Sorority Letter - and I ain't kidding. The man delivers.
6. 99 Homes. Trust me on this one.
7. Revolutionary Road
8. Nocturnal Animals

## 3. Recommend a film encouraging us to be better people

**Score:** 43
**Body:** Does such a thing exist? I know of people with anger and hatred issues who mistakenly believe making poor choices that make life harder for themselves is the way to be. What's a good film to encourage people they can be better than that.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Groundhog Day (1993)
2. Network, 1976. It'll change your life. 

Not just encourages goodness, encourages critical thinking, skepticism, etc. not the most positive outlook as a film, but will leave you desperate to change yourself for the better
3. Dave (1993)  
Article 99 (1992)  
Superman (2025)
  - If only we could have a real life Dave...
4. Social change films! 

Norma Rae (1979)

9 to 5 (1980 and there is a recent documentary about the actual movement on Netflix)

Erin Brockovich (2000)

Milk (2008)

Selma (2014)

Just Mercy (2019)
5. The Station Agent
6. Pay it forward
7. Gran Torino
8. Harold and Maude. "If you want to sing out, sing out!"
9. What dreams may come

The green mile

The pursuit of happiness

A man called Otto 

Life of pi 

The secret life of Walter Mitty 

La vita e bella 

Cast away

Click

Forest Gump

## 4. Movies about powerless woman in a miserable life

**Score:** 31
**Body:** Hi, im trying to find movies with a specific style of character. For instance:

The protagonist is a woman who's life is bleak and miserable. She is a loner and an outcast who does not fit in society. She is also powerless and unable to speak up for herself. 

Movies I have watch similar to this: 
The piano teacher (2001)
Rosetta (1999) 
The assistant (2019)
Welcome to the dollhouse (1995)
Read my lips (2001) 
The forest for the trees (2003) 
The match factory girl (1990) 




### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Revolutionary Road (2008)with Kate Winslet and Leonardo DiCaprio 

The protagonist is an unhappy 1950s housewife.
2. I Don’t Feel at Home in this World Anymore (2017)

However, she learns to take back her power.
3. Dolores Claiborne with Kathy Bates!
4. Nomadland
5. The Color Purple
6. Dancer in the dark (2000) 

I could not watch this movie in full.
7. Breaking the Waves (96)
8. Melancholia

May
9. I haven’t seen it but possibly Big Eyes?
10. The Good Girl (2002)’s protagonist perceives herself this way, but the film is kinda subtle about it.

## 5. What’s a film you’d recommend to someone who wants to get into international cinema?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** For anyone who mostly watches Hollywood movies getting into international cinema can feel a little intimidating at first but it’s such a rewarding experience, different countries have such unique ways of telling stories, and sometimes the themes, pacing or style can feel totally fresh compared to what you’re used to. Some films really open your eyes to new perspectives, cultures and emotions you might not have connected with otherwise. If someone wanted to dip their toes into international cinema for the first time what film would you recommend as the perfect starting point?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Parasite

Amélie

Cinema Paradiso

Pan's Labrinyth

Lunchbox

Piku

A Separation

Tumbad

In the Mood for Love

Blue Is the Warmest Color

La Haine

Three Colors Trilogy

The Double Life of Véronique 

Rashomon

Memories of Murder

The Handmaiden
  - You could also try Run Lola Run it’s fast paced and super engaging so it’s an easy way to get into international films
2. Really just depends on what you like.


In the mood for love: chinese romance

La chimera: italian drama

City of god: brazilian crime

The handmaiden: korean thriller
3. [The Secret in Their Eyes](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/25376-el-secreto-de-sus-ojos) (2009), Argentinian mystery thriller.
4. The Wages of Fear
5. 8 1/2   Fellini and Marcello Mastrionni.  Can't go wrong
6. Stephen Chow is the master of fun action comedies:

Kung fu hustle
Shaolin soccer
The mermaid
7. amelie
8. Day for Night (1973) - Truffaut
9. Downfall (2004)

Sophie Scholl: The Final Days

Good Bye, Lenin!

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Even after so many years later, the CGI for Davy Jones is still Impressive

**Score:** 2048

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Thats not CGI, thats a real life **octopus**
2. Should be on any list of milestone VFX achievements dating back to the beginning.
3. Genuinely groundbreaking, wish they’d give vfx artists the time and resources they need to produce similarly stunning results today.
4. Still a brilliant villain..
  - "Do you fear death...." What an opener
5. it's staggering how well both this and Curse of the Black Pearl hold up, on all fronts.
6. EXTREMELY impressive
7. Gollum crawled on the rocks of Mordor so Davy Jones could sail the high seas
8. The CGI in the original Jurassic Park is much older and holds up as well.
9. Timeless CGI 👌

## 2. Would you agree that stifler was the soul of the american pie movies?

**Score:** 967

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He’s the most memorable part for sure.  But his mom is iconic.
  - She's still playing that same character too lol.
2. Every friend group has a Stiffler. If you not sure which friend it is then you are the Stiffler.
  - i'm the sherminator
    - Ya bud, we know. Posting on Reddit is a dead giveaway
3. No. That honour goes to Stifler's Mom.
4. nah, Jim's dad, Noah Levenstein, was the real star
  - "To these guys you may be Jimbo. But you'll always be James Emmanuel Levenstein to me"

He's the best.
  - Eugene Levy has been the same age for ever.
5. He was the soul, the guy driving the bus and the highlight of all the films.  Wasn’t much funnier than Stiffler growing up.  At his best in American Wedding

## 3. What movie ending left you absolutely speechless because of how intense it was?

**Score:** 912
**Body:** I personally think the ending to "Sicario" is one of the most intense endings ever. Benicio Del Toro kind of felt like a side character throughout the whole movie. Lurking in the shadows, but that last act, holy Santa clause shit, he just took over the entire movie and his character's true, extremely dangerous, personality revealed itself, showing an unbelievably ruthless and cold-blooded man hell-bent to avenge the deaths of his wife and daughter. What an ending. "Time to meet God..." I thought about that ending for days after I saw it.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Se7en
2. The Mist. Holy crap.
3. Primal Fear.

Been chasing that high ever since.
4. Sicario does a great narrative slight of hand. We think we are watching Emily Blunts story but we are actually watching Benicios.
5. The Usual Suspects 


I don't give a fuck i can't watch it ever again but I remember i watched it years ago and I looked over at my brother and he had the same expression. What the fuck
  - WTF followed by 🤯 what an absolutely brilliant film. You couldn’t see it coming.
6. I thought I remembered another act after this scene where he catches up to the Emily Blunt character at her home and threatens her.
  - Yeah there is, when Emily Blunt’s character wisely decides not to commit suicide
7. Uncut Gems
8. Whiplash. That final drum solo. I felt like it was me on that stage, the editing and sound design is astonishing.

## 4. Waterworld wins the hated then mixed now spot. What movie was loved then but is hated now?

**Score:** 705

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The blind side
  - First movie I thought of
  - Nailed it.
  - Hated it then too. Ultimate cheesy ass white savior movie
2. Everyone is saying Crash. That was a VERY controversial best picture winner.

This one is extremely difficult. I would probably go with The Blind Side. You can’t overstate how loved that was.
3. Blind Side for me
4. Definitely the Blind Side.
5. Birth of a Nation
  - You beat me to it. This is the most polarized reception you could imagine
  - From what I remember from class, while the film made shit tons of money for its time and was maybe the first blockbuster, it also faced a ton of (deserved) backlash and disgust as well.

## 5. What's the best performance by an actress in the history of cinema, in your opinion?

**Score:** 414
**Body:** For me, no actress has come close to Meryl Streep's iconic performance in "Sophie's Choice 1982"... What about you?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jodie Foster‘s moment of realization when she realizes that she is speaking to Buffalo Bill in Silence of the Lambs is my favorite.
2. Toni Collette in The Sixth Sense  
Toni Collette in Hereditary
  - Toni Collette in Muriel's Wedding
  - Collette is fabulous. I'm so glad she's being appreciated for her work more and more. When I saw her in Muriel's Wedding, I thought she was fantastic. I didn't realize at first she also played the mother in The Sixth Sense when I saw it. Quite the chameleon.  
And...when I see her in interviews, she seems like a darling of a person, which makes me like her even more.
3. Kathy Bates in Misery
  - Kathy Bates plays mentally deranged better than any actor. Male or female or whatever gender.
4. Frances McDormand is in with a couple of good shouts.
5. Not the *best,* but Toni Collette pulls some serious chops out in The Sixth Sense. Her crying in the car near the end of the movie was spot-on.
6. Toni Collette in Hereditary.
7. There is a moment in Mask where Cher is wiring a letter to her son who is at camp.   The rawness of that moment is almost too much.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What are your thoughts on Hardcore (1979)?

**Score:** 40
**Body:** What are your thoughts on Hardcore?

I went on a George C. Scott binge recently, and I came across Hardcore. This film is really interesting, it is about a father whose teenage daughter goes missing in California and turns up in a porno film. This leads him to search for her and leads him into the subculture of pornography. 

I got to say, I honestly think this film is overlooked & underrated. What makes the film is George C. Scott and his performance as the father, Jake van Horn, who immerses himself into the industry we would never dare go into, just so that he could find his daughter. George C. Scott is just a good actor, who always commits himself to the role and always is the best part of anything he is in. Season Hubley is also great as Joanne, a prostitute who help Jake find his daughter. What I like is that Joanne and Jake found a grudging respect for each other, in spite of the cultural differences between them. Also good was Peter Boyle as the private investigator

However, I did feel that the ending was a bit of a letdown, which I found predictable. I did read that Paul Schrader was forced to change the ending from what he originally intended it to be. 

But regardless I did enjoy the film and I do think it is overlooked.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love this movie, especially the relationship between Van Horn and Joanne. When he explains to her TULIP and Calvinism is a great scene. But like you, and almost everyone else, the ending is a letdown, feels rushed, and ends the movie in a much flimsier way than you expected or hoped. Another great Scott performance though. My personal favorite is The Hospital.
2. It seems like the dark thriller "8mm" was inspired by, and perhaps copied, "Hardcore" to a significant extent. "8mm" stars Nicolas Cage as an investigator who follows a trail from the world of hardcore porn to the underground realm of snuff films. It is perhaps too dark to recommend but it does have some really good thriller scenes.
  - 8mm absolutely lifted the storyline, it just updated the violence and the salaciousness factor to modernize the vibe.

Hardcore always felt a little bit “made for tv” to me honestly, it’s very plain and even-keeled for such sensational subject matter.
3. An interesting comparison point, also, is Klute.  I think this has some similar themes (a moralising outsider detective gets a view into the world of prostitution) but also adds an compelling romantic chemistry
4. Paul Schrader's films are commonly about the failure of religious men to handle the demands of life dominated by capitalism. They've been taught that their faith will sustain them, but when it fails to, they often resort to violence of various kinds. This struggle is seen every day in the news, as evangelical pastors and Roman Catholic priests are brought to justice for abusing the women or children in their care, as domestic vilence cases explode, as men and youths reach a breaking point and commit a mass shooting.

In this story, a genial midwestern dad has to descend into the sex industry underworld in pursuit of his 15-year-old daughter, who has vanished while on a church youth trip to California.

The man, Jake VanDorn (George C. Scott), first hires a private investigator played by Peter Boyle as a two-faced, code-switching dirtbag. The PI is capable of saying the right things to the utterly square middle-class dad, but also, and clearly more naturally, to porn producers and other dirtbags in Los Angeles.

At first VanDorn waits in Grand Rapids for results. The scene where he sits forlornly on a back porch with three other men who have nothing comforting to say except things like "We can't understand God's plans" while gazing at a landfill across the road stuck in my mind for 40-plus years. These men aren't just glum because one man's daughter has vanished in California. They're Calvinists, a dour Midwestern faith that provides few opportunities for delight or amusement. In a previous scene they sat around debating humankind's depravity, not for a Bible class, but more or less for fun.

The turning point is when the PI shows VanDorn a porno featuring his daughter. VanDorn then takes matters into his own hands and jets out to California to find the girl himself.

He soon finds he can make headway only by pretending to be a porn producer himself, a plot line that provides, in one scene, the image of George C. Scott in a wig and fake mustache auditioning male porn actors for his pretend "sex action picture." With the cooperation of an actress named Niki (Season Hubley) who appeared with his daughter in the film he was shown, he finally tracks down his daughter in a San Francisco strip club.

But right before that, he encounters a trio of sex workers at a BDSM dungeon. "I'm Hope," says one of the women, motioning to the others: "and this is Faith and Charity." This is only the final ironic insertion of religious language during VanDorn's quest. Niki professes herself a Venusian -- a member of “the church of Venus.” She's pretty OK with being a sex worker, and the scenes in which she and VanDorn form an odd couple in pursuit of his daughter might serve as a basis for a comedy. And there are black-comedy moments, like the young porn director in a UCLA t-shirt who has artistic ambitions, or the porn actor who explodes into the motel room where VanDorn is holding auditions and exclaims "I'm Dick Blaque!!"

This film is quite obscure now. Only the most scrupulous fans of Paul Shrader (who also wrote Taxi Driver, another film that has child prostituion as a major plot point) are aware of it. As a director, Shrader is not flashy, and at this still early point in his career he's sinmply effective. Nevertheless, I was pleased at how well it held up after over 40 years.
  - Great write-up, I have seen the movie some time ago and you really refreshed my memory about it.
5. Good movie with a fucking amazing premise that doesn’t quite deliver the goods. It never truly goes all in on the promise of its setup and the ending is a copout. I’ve read that the more obvious ending of George C. Scott returning home with his “adopted” daughter was what Schrader intended, but the studio interfered with it. Would love to see a remake.
  - While the ending is a weak point, I think that even the intended one would be flawed. This is really a film that calls for him to head home utterly alone. Clearly and explicitly rejected by his daughter, who is much happier now living the life she wants, as well as his "adopted" daughter who will also never submit to his dour, judgmental worldview. He's morally pure, in his conception of it, but only by pushing away everything and everyone that makes him happy. Is it worth it?

It also suffers from leaning too far into the lurid pulpiness of the premise rather than following up on the undercurrent of sex positivity.
6. I recall Scott criticizing the ending and complaining that the actress cast as his daughter only got the part because she was willing to do the nude scenes. Anyway, it’s easy to point out the flaws, but I still think it’s great because of the lead performances. The cultural gap that separates them is fresh in the film and is explored really smartly.
7. Tarantino has a good review of this in his book.

Amazing beginning and middle… the end is rough and kinda spoils the movie. 

The original ending was he never finds his daughter who dies off screen in a car crash… And he takes Niki home instead. That would’ve been much better. I feel like.

Also fun fact, his daughter in that movie is played by an actual porno actress… hence why her acting is so terrible in that last scene.

## 2. Phantom Thread: Heidegger Says Yes to the Dress!

**Score:** 34
**Body:** Alma says of Reynolds's dresses: 

>I never really liked myself. I thought my shoulders were too wide, that my neck was skinny like a bird… and I had no breasts. I felt my hips were larger than needed and my arms too strong. But in his work I become perfect and I feel just right. Maybe that’s how all women feel in his clothes.

How does Reynolds achieve this effect? Although others have written about this in their own way, I couldn't help but think of Heidegger's description of the jug in *Poetry, Language, Thought*:

>When we fill the jug, the pouring that fills it flows into the empty jug. The emptiness, the void, is what does the vessel's holding. The empty space, this nothing of the jug, is what the jug is as the holding vessel.

>\[...\]

>But if the holding is done by the jug's void, then the potter who forms sides and bottom on his wheel does not, strictly speaking, make the jug. He only shapes the clay. No—he shapes the void. For it, in it, and out of it, he forms the clay into the form. From start to finish the potter takes hold of the impalpable void and brings it forth as the container in the shape of a containing vessel. The jug's void determines all the handling in the process of making the vessel. The vessel's thingness does not lie at all in the material of which it consists, but in the void that holds.

Replace any mention of the jug with a dress, and the potter with a tailor, and I think you have an apt description of Reynolds's craft.

But around what void does Reynolds form his dresses? The obvious answer is his love for his mother. Women feel beautiful in Reynolds's dresses, then, because for the short time they work with him, they get to step into that void and be seen by Reynolds just as he sees his mother: perfect.

As Richard Boothby describes Lacan's extension of Heidegger's thought:

>Lacan offers a parallel account. He poses the temple as a cardinal example of his definition of sublimation, in which "a work of art always involves encircling the Thing." The temple, Lacan says, is "a construction around emptiness that designates the place of the Thing." By means of suspending the worshipper in a hollow vacancy of space, the temple evokes "the character of a beyond of the sacred--something that we are precisely trying to identify in its most general form of the term, the Thing." In the most literal sense, the temple interior makes room for the divine.

This reading also helps make sense of Reynolds's strong negative reaction to seeing Barbara Rose in his dress: it's tantamount to sacrilege. Reynolds transforms other women into his mother, and Barbara Rose transforms his mother into a drunk.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think he might’ve got that from Lao Tzu. Here’s Ursula K. Le Guin’s translation of the Tao te Ching’s “The Uses of Not”

Hollowed out, clay makes a pot.
Where the pot's not is where it's useful.
Cut doors and windows to make a room.
Where the room isn't, there's room for you.
So the profit in what is, is in the use of what isn't.
2. Yeah… I’ve seen all but 2 of PTA movies, and [this](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lPwfENwnMlI) is my favorite one of his…

~~Khvhvjbj m h h ku mn j jh jh kn jh jh kj kublj lububj hi j h h have h h h h hkvugh h hvhvuuvhbhvhvhhvjbjbjbjbjbjbj  666~~

## 3. Tom Cruise in Iñárritu next film!

**Score:** 6
**Body:** Never in a million years did I picture Tom Cruise and Iñárritu teaming up, but I love the idea now that I have this duo in my head, I can't unsee it. Iñárritu and Cruise are both so  intense when you hear them speak about their work. They really want their films to be these massive experiences.. 

I saw Iñárritu in person once during a screening of Bardo which i loved in theater, and he spoke w/ such a raw sense of the subject as he answered questions, very intense guy - i loved it. A question asked about how he gets the actors to give these energetic performances, the questioner framed it in a way that was suggestive of the importance of plot - wish i could remember exact wording - but he was like (paraphrasing) 'to hell with the plot, that stuff is non sense' saying the emotion comes out of moments unconsciously with no understanding, life is not comprehensible, etc. 

As a screenwriter I don’t fully agree with that, but I admire him for being this renegade voice, still carrying that energy at 62 years old.

And the premise of this new movie feels like exactly what Tom Cruise is made for. After Iñárritu scratched his Terrence Malick / Tarkovsky itch with Bardo and The Revenant, it’s exciting to see him move back toward something more plot-driven. Feels like he’s more free now to go make a crowd-pleaser again.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Maybe you should repost this once the film is actually released and you’ve seen it. There isn’t anything to analyze or discuss here since no one’s seen it! Maybe it’s not very good. Maybe it’s a triumph. This isn’t really the appropriate time or place!
  - What do you mean? He already saw the movie in his head
    - He’s comparing Iñárritu with Tarkovsky and has a hard-on for Sam Harris.
2. That set is going to be something else. Two huge control freak egos battling it out. Tom Hardy punching Iñárritu (allegedly) on The Revenant will look like child's play. I'm trying to imagine it and I can't. 

Don't get me wrong, OP, I am also very interested to see this combo and I think they may make something interesting, but oh my god the process is going to be legendary.
  - Revenant also had a lot of instances of Inarittu and Lubezki abusing the crap out of below-the-line crew members. Countless people quit because they couldn’t put up with the torturous working conditions. 

I don’t care for their work. If you can’t make your art without hurting other people, then you have no right to make art. I, for one, will not be lining up to watch this. I’m not interested in supporting the work of abusive people.
  - It's already completed filming with no reported production issues

## 4. Sunrise: A Song of Two Humans

**Score:** 4
**Body:** What is it about this film that has led to it being ranked one of the greatest not merely of the silent era but of all time? I'm struggling to see it for myself, but my instinct when something like this happens is to try and understand what the people who champion the film as a masterpiece are seeing. Personally, I wouldn't even rank it as Murnau's best: his visual style doesn't excite me here as much as it did in *The Last Laugh* and *Faust*, and narratively/thematically it feels very slight compared to those films. What am I missing?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Because it’s the first cinematic fable. There’s something spiritually powerful about this film. Murnau pares everything down to something deeply primal: desire, guilt, forgiveness, love. in a way that transcends its time and still hits the soul today.

I guess how you connect to the story and characters will always be subjective, but even on a purely technical level, Sunrise was groundbreaking. The visuals, camera work and editing were revolutionary. The way the camera glides through spaces, the dissolves, the dreamlike transitions all of it influenced generations of filmmakers and helped shape the very fundamental language of cinema.
2. It was pretty much head above of 90% of American silent dramas in terms of storytelling clarity and technical innovation. The story is basic but it is told so smoothly with so much grace it is ridiculous. It shouldn't work but Murnau makes it look easy.
3. Literally nothing. It's quite possibly the most overrated movie of all time. It's not even the best one from 1927, too. I mean, come on, "The Unknown" blows it out of the water and you'd have to be really blind not to think that.

People who claim this is one of the bestest movies there is simply haven't touched much cinema from the 20s. Because if they had, they'd see this shit for what it is: nothing outstanding.

Even if were to rank it within its genre: 7th heaven is a much better romance, from the same year.
  - You said nothing beyond the movie being overrated. For what reason? 

I agree that 1927 had far more notable and better films, like Napoleon, The Lodger and Metropolis but that’s not to say that Sunrise is a bad movie. I got to see it at a local theatre with my girlfriend (who’s never seen a silent movie) and much of the romantic elements still hits as we can still see the chemistry through their silent acting. I do think it’s beautifully made and acted, with some of the effects and night shots especially being notable to me. 

Just because it’s not loud and in your face does not mean it’s a lesser movie.
    - It's nicely shot. It looks good. That's pretty much all the positives I can muster.

The story? Nothing interesting and outright annoying, since it's so obvious. The characters? One-dimensional cut-outs. The plot? I could write smth like that in less than half an hour, it's so obvious and basic.

Again, I think the "consensus classic" status is at play here. No shot your girlfriend would hold it in high regard were she to watch it back-to-back with '7th heaven" and "The Unknown". It's just a nicely shot, obvious, one-dimensional, boring-ass romance flick.
  - I have seen hundreds of silent films and consider it my favorite. I find it incredibly moving and communicative and beautiful. Just because people feel differently than you doesn’t mean they’re ignorant.

## 5. The Breakfast Club & the Negative Effects of Neglect

**Score:** 0
**Body:** The Breakfast Club celebrates its 40th anniversary this year.

Surely, the film has had a lasting cultural impact, remaining one of the most beloved "coming of age" films of the 1980's. However, unlike the other movies of its era, The Breakfast Club dared to confront one massively overlooked problem in American society--the epidemic of childhood neglect. Through the character of Allison, the film shed light on the immense damage which is inflicted on a child when they are ignored. And truly, in a culture with very narrow definitions of child abuse, this was boundary-pushing.

Societally, we often turn a blind eye to the harm done to children--aside from the obvious, glaring examples of physical abuse and the like. But, truly, there are many ways to hurt a child--you can hit them, scream at them, deprive them of food and shelter.

Or, you can simply ignore them. Just as Allison was ignored.

Check out my DEEP-DIVE into this vulnerable and complex character. It's a fascinating analysis that any fan of this film (or anyone neglected in childhood) will find great value in: [https://youtu.be/R6UO1LPI4\_E](https://youtu.be/R6UO1LPI4_E)

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


---
